## [00:00 - 03:00]

Now we'll be diving into the Browse abandonment. But real quickly, before we talk about the Browse abandonment, I wanted to point out that there's actually three different types of abandonment emails. So one is Browse abandonment, which is when someone on your list that you have their email for, it's just casually viewing a product, but they don't actually add to their cart. The second is the abandoned cart which most people seem to know, and that's when someone on your list adds to the cart, but doesn't start to check out. And then the last one's actually called abandoned checkout, and this is when someone's brought your product, added to cart, started to check out, but didn't make their purchase. So these three will be walking to each of them individually, and they're all really helpful for driving revenue. Typically when I audit potential clients' emails, most of them are not even doing any of these, or if they are, they're only doing one, typically because they don't know of the others. If you don't have these flows, you're basically leaving an additional couple thousand dollars per month on the table. Most of our clients that are doing six, seven, and eight figures are doing anywhere from about two to four, or three to five thousand dollars for each of these flows. One other thing worth noting, so as mentioned, Clavio is my personal favorite email platform. Clavio doesn't really make two of these, two of these three that easy to do. By default, you'll have the abandoned checkout, but you don't have the abandoned cart or the Browse abandonment. So you'll actually have to add snippets of code to your website. Don't be afraid if you don't know how to add this snippet of code. I've provided documentation for how you can add it to your store. It's pretty simple to do, and we'll try to create a video as well, if needed, on how you can actually do this yourself. So a couple of high level tips for personalization is make sure that when you do these emails that you dynamically display the product itself, and we'll walk through how to do that. But the key is making sure that you're showing people the products that they either looked at, that they added to their cart, or they started to checkout. This will remind people what they were browsing or looking at, and this will most likely get them to come back. And again, as mentioned in the Well Well example, it's really important to kind of call out the pain points that you're solving for. So with Well Well, if someone was sore, then they should go for the muscle product. So in your abandoned cart, if someone's looking at the protein option, remind them that this will help with their muscle soreness. So it's really important to highlight the value that your product brings the customer to help drive the sale. And again, make it really easy for the customer to come back with all the links that you can create. It will take them back to the product that they were viewing, or it will take them back to their cart, or it will take them back to the checkout. So make sure you're leveraging those links so that way the customer doesn't have to go through the whole process again. So diving into the first abandonment series is the browse abandonment. And again, as a reminder, if someone's already on your email list and views a product, but doesn't add it to a cart, this will trigger the browse. So again, everything for all these emails are based off triggers. Someone views a product, they'll then receive this browse abandonment. The point of the browse abandonment, and the reason why it's so effective, is this helps drive people down your sales funnel. So people obviously have entered their email,

## [03:00 - 06:00]

so there's some intent. They've viewed products, whether it's casually or with intent. So this will help them move farther down the funnel and ultimately try to drive the sale. The browse abandonment's gonna work for you while you're busy doing whatever you're doing. One thing worth noting is the browse abandonment emails typically have the highest open rate of all the other flows. And they also have a higher open rate than all your campaigns typically do. So on my screen right here, you can see kind of at the top where it says the trigger, that this is when someone views product. So after two hours of someone viewing a product, it will then trigger this email. So you can see here, we have four different emails. Typically I recommend two or three. The reason we got up to four is we started with two, thought that we could drive more revenue. And kind of at a high level, again, these all have where it called flow filters. And basically this is a filter that will boot someone from the flow itself, or it's basically the criteria that allows people to get in. And with this, there's three of them. So one of them is if someone makes a purchase after any of these emails, they'll stop receiving the other emails. You don't want to annoy customers with receiving too many emails. So if they purchase after the first email, you don't want to send them three others. They'll then go to a post purchase as mentioned. So one other thing that you can leverage to make sure that people aren't getting annoyed and not receiving too many of your emails on a daily basis is leveraging in Clavio in particular, what's called smart sending. And basically what smart sending does is it allows you to set the number of hours that people will not receive more than one email within. So typically it's normally ranges from 16 to 24 hours. So if someone received a campaign email today, and they were supposed to also receive a browser email because it looked at that product but they didn't add to their cart, smart sending would prevent them from receiving that browser email today. Because again, we want to make sure that people don't get too many emails in one day. Now you work so hard to get people on your list. We want to retain them, convert them, and have them as customers for a long time. So we're trying to avoid having them unsubscribed. And then the other filter is around added to cart and started checkout. And what I mean by that is, so the browser abandonment, if you think about a customer, right? So they entered your list and they're viewing products. They're interested, but they're not as far down the funnel as abandoned cart or an abandoned checkout. So these filters basically say if someone's added to their cart or they started checkout, do not send them this browser abandonment because we want to prioritize other emails

## [09:01 - 12:01]

So we're actually extending your discount code. And you noticed, I think in the flow overview that this email drove the most amount of revenue. So you'll notice here that the same trigger was being used again around viewed product. That trigger is never gonna change. I'll always be the same for the browse abandonment. And then you'll notice here what's different on this client is that we're leveraging a conditional split. And essentially what a conditional split in this case does is it splits the traffic into two. Version A gets one time, version B gets another time. So in this case, the people that receive the first email in version A, they're gonna receive it after one hour. Whereas the people on the right hand side in version B, they're gonna start receiving that content after two hours. The reason that we do this is everyone has a different conversion rate depending on the time delay. Some clients we work with send this browse abandonment after one hour and other clients see it better performance after four hours. So you can see here that after two hours of sending this first email, there's slightly a higher place order rate of 1.2% after two hours versus 1% after one hour. And again, you can see the revenue here is pretty substantial. The left side has driven about $1,000 for revenue and the right side has driven $1,200 of revenue.
